perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall treat all Confidential Information with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall endeavor to treat all Confidential Information with a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation to treat confidential information with the same level of care as one's own. The modified text introduces ambiguity with 'endeavor' and 'reasonable degree,' weakening the obligation and potentially failing to meet required standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the GDPR mandates appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including protecting against accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure of, or access to, personal data. The modified text, by using 'endeavor' and 'reasonable degree,' introduces subjectivity and reduces the assurance of data protection, potentially falling short of the GDPR's requirement for 'appropriate' and demonstrably effective security measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting GDPR Article 32. The original text mandates treating confidential information with the same care as one's own, while the changed text uses 'endeavor' and 'reasonable degree,' introducing subjectivity. Art. 32 GDPR requires 'appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk'. The shift to 'endeavor' and 'reasonable degree' lessens the assurance of data protection, potentially falling short of the GDPR's requirement for 'appropriate' security measures, creating an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored) , including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed un der this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Disclosing Party may r equest it to the Receiving Party .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall make good faith efforts to return or destroy copies of the Confidential Information, as reasonably practical, after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates prompt return or destruction of all copies. The modified text weakens this by adding 'make good faith efforts' and 'as reasonably practical,' which introduces subjectivity and allows for interpretation on what constitutes a sufficient effort, especially since the original language stipulates that this must happen even before termination of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4(2) of Directive 2016/943 requires member states to ensure that competent judicial authorities may, on application by the trade secret holder, order the cessation of or, as the case may be, the prohibition of the use or disclosure of the trade secret. The modified text's weakening of the obligation to return or destroy confidential information undermines the protection of trade secrets provided under the directive, as it creates loopholes where the Receiving Party may not fully comply with the destruction or return of confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original's clear directive for prompt return or destruction of confidential information. By introducing 'make good faith efforts' and 'as reasonably practical,' the obligation is weakened and subjective, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that Directive 2016/943 intends to ensure competent authorities can order the cessation or prohibition of the use or disclosure of trade secrets. The modified text creates ambiguity about when and how confidential information must be returned or destroyed, and therefore creates a loophole. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies Directive (EU) 2016/943 as being 'on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure,' demonstrating the pertinence of the directive in this instance.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party is asked to communicate the Confidential I n- formation to any judicial, administrative, regulatory authority or similar or obliged to reveal such information by mandatory law, it shall notify promptly the Disclosing Party o f the terms of such disclosure and will collaborate to the extent practicable with the Di s- closing Party in order to comply with the order and preserve the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Should the Receiving Party be requested to communicate Confidential Information to a judicial or regulatory authority under mandatory law, the Receiving Party should inform the Disclosing Party when feasible and consider collaborating on compliance.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires prompt notification and collaboration to preserve confidentiality. The modified text weakens this by changing 'shall notify promptly' to 'should inform when feasible' and 'will collaborate to the extent practicable' to 'consider collaborating,' creating ambiguity and making compliance optional. The changes shift the responsibility from an obligation to something that may or may not happen.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4(5) of Directive 2016/943 states that measures, procedures and remedies provided for in this Directive shall not restrict the exercise of the fundamental rights and freedoms, including the freedom of expression and information, as enshrined in the Charter, including respect for freedom and pluralism of the media. The modified text makes the obligation to 'inform the Disclosing Party' optional, which hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to exercise their right to protect their trade secrets in legal proceedings. By weakening the obligation to inform promptly and collaborate, it impedes the trade secret holder's ability to take appropriate legal actions to protect their rights.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to promptly notify and collaborate, making compliance optional. The original text 'shall notify promptly' and 'will collaborate to the extent practicable' are mandatory, whereas the changed text uses 'should inform when feasible' and 'consider collaborating,' which introduces ambiguity. The Perturbation Explanation states this shift makes compliance optional. The Law Explanation notes that this hinders the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Directive (EU) 2016/943, which aims to protect trade secrets against unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure. The modified text undermines this protection by weakening the notification and collaboration requirements, thus creating a legal contradiction because it reduces the ability of the Disclosing Party to protect their Trade Secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreeme nt shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years ]. Notwithstanding the foregoi ng, the Receiving Party’s duty to hold in confidence Conf i- dential Information that was disclosed during the term shal l remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a commercially reasonable term. The Receiving Party’s duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall continue as long as it seems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a defined term with an indefinite confidentiality obligation. The modified text replaces the defined term with 'commercially reasonable term,' which is vague and subject to interpretation. It also replaces 'indefinitely' with 'as long as it seems appropriate,' introducing further ambiguity and making the duration of confidentiality subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: While UTSA doesn't explicitly mandate a specific timeframe for confidentiality agreements, it protects trade secrets for as long as they remain trade secrets and derive independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified text creates uncertainty about the duration of protection, undermining the 'reasonable efforts' standard implicitly required to maintain trade secret status. A 'commercially reasonable term' or 'as long as it seems appropriate' may not adequately protect information for the duration it qualifies as a trade secret under UTSA, potentially leading to loss of trade secret status due to inadequate protection measures.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the original text's defined term and indefinite confidentiality obligation with 'commercially reasonable term' and 'as long as it seems appropriate,' respectively. This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity regarding the duration of confidentiality. The Law Explanation states this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' standard implicitly required to maintain trade secret status under UTSA, as the modified terms may not adequately protect information for the duration it qualifies as a trade secret. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to Terms & Conditions and Privacy & Cookie Policy. While not directly quoted, this snippet points to the existence of enforceable policies which the change to 'commercially reasonable term' and 'as long as it seems appropriate' potentially violates, leading to a direct and serious contradiction from a legal perspective as it weakens the protection afforded to confidential information.
